overview: From 2026-06-10 to 2026-06-11, elizaos/eliza had 26 new PRs (12 merged), 1 new issues, and 8 active contributors.

topIssues.title: android: clean-tree builds ship web-less APKs — cap sync and gradle operate on different projects (since 7ac0e1fb96)
topIssues.author: NubsCarson
topIssues.body:
## Symptom
`bun run --cwd packages/app build:android` from a clean tree produces an APK with `assets/agent` but **zero `assets/public`, no `capacitor.config.json`** → on device the WebView shows `https://localhost/ net::ERR_CONNECTION_REFUSED` forever (no Capacitor `errorPath` is configured, so there's no retry). Verified on a Solana Seeker tonight.

## Root cause
`7ac0e1fb96` (fix(rv64-pipeline), May 20) moved `androidDir` in `packages/app-core/scripts/run-mobile-build.mjs:89` from `packages/app/android` → `packages/app-core/platforms/android` (gradle, native patches, agent staging) — but `runCapacitor(["sync","android"])` still runs with cwd `packages/app` and `capacitor.config.ts` has **no `android.path`**, so the Capacitor CLI keeps delivering the web layer to `packages/app/android`. Nothing bridges the two trees: `capacitor-platform-templates.mjs:27-43` explicitly skips `assets/public`, `assets/agent`, and the generated capacitor JSONs when materializing templates.

Evidence it used to be one project: the tracked `packages/app-core/platforms/android/capacitor.settings.gradle` projectDirs (`../../../app/node_modules/…`) are only producible with the platform dir at `app-core/platforms/android` — i.e. an `android.path` override existed and was lost. A pre-May-20 develop worktree (`bb69a3642e`) has all five asset trees together under `packages/app/android`. Same asymmetry: `iosDir` lives in the host app (`packages/app/ios/App`), android was left behind.

## Why CI doesn't catch it
- `build-android.yml` / `mobile-build-smoke.yml` only assert the APK **file exists** (smoke: lines 255-262); the artifact audits (`auditAndroidCloudArtifact`) check banned entries only, never that `assets/public/index.html` is present.
- No workflow boots the app; `mobile-local-chat-smoke.mjs` isn't wired into any workflow.
- Builds on machines with stale untracked `assets/public` leftovers in the platforms tree appear to work — clean checkouts never do.

## Verified workaround (tonight, on-device confirmed)
After `build:android`: copy `packages/app/android/app/src/main/assets/{public,capacitor.config.json,capacitor.plugins.json}` + `res/xml/config.xml` into `packages/app-core/platforms/android/app/src/main/...`, re-run `./gradlew :app:assembleDebug` there → complete APK (282→336MB), app boots to onboarding on a real device. Caveat: the tracked canonical `capacitor.settings.gradle` lags the synced one (missing background-runner, barcode-scanner, browser, device, haptics, network plugin projects → runtime "plugin not implemented" degradation).

## Proposed fix (either)
1. Restore the lost wiring: `android: { path: "../app-core/platforms/android" }` in `packages/app/capacitor.config.ts` (and teach `ensure-capacitor-platform.mjs`/`isCapacitorPlatformReady` to probe that dir), so sync/copy/update land directly in the gradle tree; or
2. Add a `stageAndroidWebAssets()` step after every `runCapacitor(["sync","android"])` call (4 android targets) copying the web payload + regenerated capacitor gradle files (with path rewrite) into `androidDir`.

Plus, regardless: a **positive APK audit** for sideload targets (assert `assets/public/index.html` + `assets/capacitor.config.json` + `assets/agent/` via `jar tf`) and the same one-liner in CI after assembleDebug, so a web-less APK can never ship silently again.

Found while device-testing the PR #8340 APK; happy to PR whichever shape you prefer — flagging first since the mobile pipeline is active WIP.

topPRs.title: refactor(infra): split apps-data-plane → apps-shared + per-env workers
topPRs.author: standujar
topPRs.body:
## Why

The tenant Postgres + private network are physically a single shared piece of
infra across staging + production app workers, but they were being modelled
per-env inside `apps-data-plane/`. That meant a staging vs prod apply could in
theory rebuild the tenant DB out from under the other env, and the two state
files each thought they owned the same Hetzner-side network. Splitting the
module pins the truth in TF.

## What changed

### New `apps-shared/` module — single shared backend

Owns the resources that are physically one thing:

- `hcloud_network.apps` + `hcloud_network_subnet.apps` (`10.30.0.0/16` → `10.30.1.0/24`)
- `hcloud_firewall.tenant_db` (port 22 from operator CIDRs)
- `hcloud_volume.tenant_db_data` (200 GB default)
- `hcloud_server.tenant_db` (`cpx42` default, **named `eliza-app-tenant`** — no env suffix because the resource is shared)
- `hcloud_server_network.tenant_db` (`10.30.1.10` private IP, stable)
- `hcloud_volume_attachment.tenant_db_data`
- `random_password.tenant_db_admin`
- `cloud-init/tenant-db.yaml.tftpl` (copied verbatim from apps-data-plane — latest fixed version with the bootcmd + systemd-oneshot ordering)
- `versions.tf` / `providers.tf` / `variables.tf` / `outputs.tf`
- `backend.hcl` (single shared backend file → `hetzner/apps-shared/shared.tfstate`)
- `tfvars/shared.tfvars.example`

Outputs: `apps_network_id`, `apps_subnet_id`, `apps_subnet_cidr`, `tenant_db_private_ip` (10.30.1.10), `tenant_db_public_ip`, `tenant_db_admin_dsn` (sensitive).

Note: I didn't move the `app_node` firewall into shared — it's per-env (its name carries the env suffix and the operator CIDR list could legitimately diverge between staging and prod), so the split stays clean: shared = the actual shared infra, per-env = everything env-scoped.

### Updated `apps-data-plane/` — slimmer, per-env

- Drops every tenant_db resource + the network/subnet.
- Adds `data "terraform_remote_state" "apps_shared"` pointing at the shared R2 state file.
- `hcloud_server_network.app_node` attaches to `data.terraform_remote_state.apps_shared.outputs.apps_network_id`.
- `cloud-init/app-node.yaml.tftpl` keeps its `tenant_db_host` template var, now sourced from the remote state output.
- Outputs no longer include any `tenant_db_*` field.
- Dropped the `random` provider (no longer used) so `.terraform.lock.hcl` shrinks.

### Workflows

- **NEW** `.github/workflows/terraform-apps-shared.yml` — `pull_request` (fmt + validate) + `workflow_dispatch` with `action` (plan/apply) and optional `replace` input. **No environment input** — module owns one set of resources. Uses repo-level vars `APPS_SHARED_OPERATOR_SSH_KEY` + `APPS_SHARED_OPERATOR_INGRESS_CIDRS`.
- **UPDATED** `.github/workflows/terraform-apps-data-plane.yml` — same per-env dispatch shape; header explains tenant_db lives elsewhere now; `replace` notice mentions `hcloud_server.tenant_db` is a no-op here (use the apps-shared workflow). R2 state creds still cover both this module's state AND the apps-shared remote-state read.

### Misc

- ARCHITECTURE.md updated: tree shows `eliza-app-tenant` shared once, the per-env list only carries app_node servers.
- `apps-data-plane/README.md` rewritten around the split + the apply order.

## State migration plan — operator runs ONCE, manually

There's existing state in `hetzner/apps-data-plane/staging.tfstate` (the tenant DB was deployed on staging before this refactor). Move it to the new shared backend before any new apply.

Order of operations:

1. **Pause both workflows on develop** so nobody apply-races the move:
   ```
   gh workflow disable terraform-apps-data-plane.yml
   gh workflow disable terraform-apps-shared.yml
   ```

2. **Pull both state files locally**:
   ```bash
   export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=...    # R2_STATE_ACCESS_KEY_ID
   export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=...
   export HCLOUD_TOKEN=...         # HCLOUD_APPS_TOKEN

   # source: staging apps-data-plane (pre-refactor layout)
   cd packages/cloud-infra/cloud/terraform/hetzner/apps-data-plane
   git checkout develop  # the OLD code path, with tenant_db in this module
   terraform init -backend-config=backend-staging.hcl
   terraform state pull > /tmp/staging-old.tfstate

   # destination: brand-new apps-shared backend (empty)
   cd ../apps-shared
   git checkout refactor/apps-shared-module
   terraform init -backend-config=backend.hcl
   ```

3. **Move the tenant DB + deps from the old staging state into the new shared state**. Run these from the `apps-shared/` working directory (`-state` reads source from disk, `-state-out` writes destination):
   ```bash
   terraform state mv -state=/tmp/staging-old.tfstate -state-out=/tmp/shared-new.tfstate \
     hcloud_network.apps                   hcloud_network.apps
   terraform state mv -state=/tmp/staging-old.tfstate -state-out=/tmp/shared-new.tfstate \
     hcloud_network_subnet.apps            hcloud_network_subnet.apps
   terraform state mv -state=/tmp/staging-old.tfstate -state-out=/tmp/shared-new.tfstate \
     hcloud_firewall.tenant_db             hcloud_firewall.tenant_db
   terraform state mv -state=/tmp/staging-old.tfstate -state-out=/tmp/shared-new.tfstate \
     hcloud_volume.tenant_db_data          hcloud_volume.tenant_db_data
   terraform state mv -state=/tmp/staging-old.tfstate -state-out=/tmp/shared-new.tfstate \
     hcloud_server.tenant_db               hcloud_server.tenant_db
   terraform state mv -state=/tmp/staging-old.tfstate -state-out=/tmp/shared-new.tfstate \
     hcloud_server_network.tenant_db       hcloud_server_network.tenant_db
   terraform state mv -state=/tmp/staging-old.tfstate -state-out=/tmp/shared-new.tfstate \
     hcloud_volume_attachment.tenant_db_data hcloud_volume_attachment.tenant_db_data
   terraform state mv -state=/tmp/staging-old.tfstate -state-out=/tmp/shared-new.tfstate \
     random_password.tenant_db_admin       random_password.tenant_db_admin
   ```

4. **Push both updated state files**:
   ```bash
   # write the new shared state
   cd packages/cloud-infra/cloud/terraform/hetzner/apps-shared
   terraform state push /tmp/shared-new.tfstate

   # write the slimmed-down staging state back
   cd ../apps-data-plane
   terraform state push /tmp/staging-old.tfstate
   ```

5. **Verify both modules plan against reality**:
   ```bash
   cd ../apps-shared && terraform plan -var-file=shared.tfvars
   # EXPECTED (apply this — these are the intended renames + label updates):
   #   ~ hcloud_network.apps        — labels: tier "apps-data-plane" -> "apps-shared",
   #                                  drop environment key (NAME rename suppressed via
   #                                  lifecycle.ignore_changes=[name])
   #   ~ hcloud_volume.tenant_db_data   — same labels diff; name rename suppressed
   #   ~ hcloud_firewall.tenant_db      — same labels diff; name rename suppressed
   #   ~ hcloud_server.tenant_db        — same labels diff; name + user_data +
   #                                       image + ssh_keys all suppressed
   # All four diffs are IN-PLACE (Hetzner PATCH on labels), no destroy, volume +
   # data + private IP all stable. Run `terraform apply` once to converge.
   #
   # NOT EXPECTED (abort + rollback if you see any of these):
   #   - hcloud_server.tenant_db destroyed / replaced
   #   - hcloud_volume.tenant_db_data destroyed / replaced
   #   - any "+ -> destroy" line

   cd ../apps-data-plane && terraform plan -var-file=staging.tfvars
   # expected: no changes — app_node + firewall + DNS already in this state,
   # the new data source just reads the (now shared) network id.
   ```

6. **Re-enable workflows**:
   ```
   gh workflow enable terraform-apps-data-plane.yml
   gh workflow enable terraform-apps-shared.yml
   ```

7. **Done.** Future applies of `apps-data-plane` for staging and production both consume the same shared state.

### About the Hetzner-side name (`eliza-apps-tenantdb-staging` → `eliza-app-tenant`)

The existing tenant DB Hetzner server is named `eliza-apps-tenantdb-staging`. After the state move, TF wants the resource called `eliza-app-tenant` — but `hcloud_server.tenant_db.lifecycle.ignore_changes` already includes `name`, so **TF will NOT rename the server Hetzner-side**. The operator can rename it via the Hetzner Console (cosmetic only — labels, IPs, volumes are all stable).

## Rollback plan

If anything goes wrong after the state move but before reality changes:

1. Revert this PR (`git revert` on develop).
2. Restore the staging state from the R2 versioned bucket (R2 keeps prior object versions — the pre-move tfstate is still there as the previous version).
3. Or push `/tmp/staging-old.tfstate.bak` back (`terraform state push -force` from the backup we kept locally before step 4).

The tenant DB itself is never destroyed during this migration — `terraform state mv` only edits the state graph, no apply runs against the cloud. Worst case after a botched revert is "state thinks the tenant DB is in one module, reality has it; next apply tries to recreate it" → operator rolls back state instead, no DB loss.

## Test plan

- [x] `terraform fmt -check -recursive` clean in both modules
- [x] `terraform init -backend=false && terraform validate` succeeds in both modules
- [x] Both workflows parse as valid YAML
- [ ] PR CI: validate job passes for both `terraform-apps-shared.yml` and `terraform-apps-data-plane.yml`
- [ ] Operator runs the state migration plan above on staging
- [ ] `apps-shared` `plan` returns no changes after the state move
- [ ] `apps-data-plane` staging `plan` returns no changes after the state move
- [ ] Then `apps-data-plane` production `plan` (first apply against this module for prod) shows only the **new** app_node + firewall + DNS for production, no tenant_db touch

## Notes

- No flag toggles — `apps-data-plane` no longer has a `provision_tenant_db` knob, the split is hard.
- No legacy fallback path — the old per-env tenant_db code is gone from `apps-data-plane/`. The state move is the only path forward.
- `app_node` firewall stayed in `apps-data-plane` (per-env), only the tenant_db firewall moved to shared. If we ever want the app_node firewall shared too it's a separate refactor.

topPRs.title: feat(cloud): add Atlas Cloud image provider (drop-in, OpenAI-compatible)
topPRs.author: 0xSolace
topPRs.body:
## Why
The deployed cloud worker routes image models (gpt-image-2, gemini-image) through **BitRouter**, but BitRouter serves **no image models** — its `/v1/models` catalog is 100% text-output. So `generate-image` 500s. **Atlas Cloud** does serve `gpt-image-2` + the gemini image models, with native (un-marked-up) pricing and $0 per-request fees.

This adds Atlas as a **one-file-style provider** using the image-provider abstraction from #8064 — so **Shaw just drops in `ATLASCLOUD_API_KEY` and it works**, and we can test with our key now.

## Changes
- **`providers/image/atlascloud-image-generation.ts`** (new): OpenAI-compatible chat-completions executor. Mirrors the proven BitRouter image-via-chat shape (`modalities:["image","text"]`, extracts `choices[0].message.images[0].image_url`). Base URL defaults to `https://api.atlascloud.ai/v1` (override `ATLASCLOUD_BASE_URL`); key from `ATLASCLOUD_API_KEY`.
- **`providers/image/registry.ts`**: register `atlasCloudImageProvider`.
- **`ai-pricing-definitions.ts`**: add `"atlascloud"` to `PricingBillingSource` + two **Atlas-only** `SUPPORTED_IMAGE_MODELS` (`openai/gpt-image-2`, `google/gemini-3.1-flash-image`) — new modelIds, no collision with existing BitRouter entries.
- **`ai-pricing/providers/atlascloud.ts` + `gateway.ts`**: per-image pricing seed (conservative manual estimates, flagged to refine with account-specific pricing).
- **`generate-image` routes** (app + non-app): thread `ATLASCLOUD_API_KEY`/`ATLASCLOUD_BASE_URL` into `apiKeys` + add the 503-on-missing-key gate, mirroring bitrouter/fal.
- **`cloud-worker-env.ts`**: optional `ATLASCLOUD_API_KEY` / `ATLASCLOUD_BASE_URL`.

## Drop-in
Set **`ATLASCLOUD_API_KEY`** and Atlas image models work. Everything else has sane defaults. Existing BitRouter/fal entries are untouched.

## Notes / follow-ups
- The exact Atlas image-request payload mirrors BitRouter's proven OpenAI-image-via-chat shape (Atlas exposes the same surface). Live image generation should be smoke-tested once the Atlas account is funded; text completions are confirmed working.
- Pricing rows are conservative seeds — refine with account-specific Atlas pricing before relying on exact margins.
- Separately: this provider abstraction (#8064) + this PR need to actually be **deployed** to the prod cloud worker (the live bundle still has the old hardcoded BitRouter path).

Co-authored-by: wakesync <shadow@shad0w.xyz>

topPRs.title: fix(infra): tenant-db cloud-init -> systemd oneshot (idempotent, survives rescale)
topPRs.author: standujar
topPRs.body:
## Summary

Wraps the tenant-DB cluster setup in a systemd oneshot service installed via
`write_files`. The service runs on **every boot** (not just the first one),
every step is grep- or path-guarded so it's a no-op when there's nothing to
do.

## Why

After the cpx41/cpx42 rescale this morning (PR #8379), tenant_db came up with
postgres dead. The volume mounted fine but PGDATA never existed:

| Check | Observed |
|---|---|
| `systemctl is-active postgresql@16-main` | `inactive (dead)` |
| `pg_lsclusters` | empty |
| `df -h /mnt/tenant-pgdata` | 32K used (freshly formatted) |
| `ls /mnt/tenant-pgdata/16/main/` | no such file or directory |
| Cloud-init log on rescaled boot | finished in <1s, runcmd absent |

Root cause: cloud-init `runcmd` is per-instance (first boot only). Hetzner
rescale = stop → change type → start, same instance id, so runcmd never
fires again.

## Fixes inside the migration

A few latent bugs in the old `runcmd` script that this version closes:

- fstab append wasn't grep-guarded → duplicate lines on every re-run
- `pg_hba` `hostssl` / `host` lines weren't grep-guarded → same
- `postgresql.conf` seds only matched the commented form (`^#listen_addresses`)
  → silently failed on re-runs after the first sed had uncommented them
- PGDATA check used `! -d` → true for an empty directory left behind by a
  half-finished initdb. Now checks `! -f $PGDATA/PG_VERSION`
- `mkfs.ext4 -F` removed → the `blkid` gate is the only protection; `-F`
  would silently nuke a real fs if `blkid` ever lied
- ALTER USER postgres now waits for `pg_isready` before firing (was racing)
- Script `set -euo pipefail` so partial failures don't masquerade as success

## Workflow input

Adds an optional `replace` input to `terraform-apps-data-plane.yml` —
comma-separated TF resource addresses, threaded as `-replace=<addr>` flags.

Reason: `user_data` lives in `hcloud_server.tenant_db.lifecycle.ignore_changes`
(matches the control-plane convention, `ARCHITECTURE.md` calls this out
explicitly). So a normal apply won't replace the existing tenant_db to pick
up the fixed cloud-init. The `replace` input scratches that itch without
having to taint via local terraform CLI access.

Reusable: any future "rebuild this resource cleanly" follows the same path —
dispatch with `replace="hcloud_server.foo"`, no taint gymnastics.

## Test plan

- [x] `terraform fmt -recursive` clean
- [x] `terraform validate` green
- [ ] CI: PR validate job green
- [ ] After merge, dispatch with `environment=staging action=apply replace=hcloud_server.tenant_db`
- [ ] Verify the new tenant_db comes up with `systemctl is-active postgresql` = `active`, `pg_lsclusters` shows the 16/main cluster, port 5432 listening on the private net
- [ ] Then load the 2 user DBs from the OLD CP dump (separate, manual `psql -f`)

topPRs.title: feat(infra): cloud-init bakes nginx vhost + self-signed cert + CF proxied=true
topPRs.author: standujar
topPRs.body:
## Why

Staging migration today landed a TF-spawned control-plane VM that was unroutable for agent traffic:

- nginx ran the default Ubuntu vhost on :80 (no proxy_pass to agent-router on \`127.0.0.1:3458\`)
- :443 had no listener at all
- CF DNS record \`eliza-staging-1.elizacloud.ai\` was \`proxied=false\` (grey cloud), so CF Workers fetching it went direct → got 521 on missing :443

Repaired manually on the live VM. This PR bakes the same setup into the TF module so the next apply (prod, or any re-spawn) lands a routable VM out of the box.

## What

**cloud-init/bootstrap.yaml.tftpl**:
- New \`/etc/nginx/sites-available/eliza-agent-router\` vhost (mirrors prod CP setup) — \`*.elizacloud.ai → 127.0.0.1:3458\` on :80 + :443
- Generate self-signed \`*.elizacloud.ai\` cert at boot via \`openssl req -x509\` → \`/etc/nginx/ssl/eliza-agent-router.{crt,key}\`
- Drop the default vhost + enable the new one + reload nginx
- Git clone retries 3× with 5s sleep — silent race with apt-installed \`git\` left /opt/eliza empty on a slow image

**main.tf**:
- \`cloudflare_dns_record.control_plane.proxied = true\` + \`ttl = 1\` (CF API requires Auto TTL when proxied)
- Comment explains why direct origin fetch fails on self-signed cert

## Why self-signed is fine

Zone SSL on \`elizacloud.ai\` is set to "Full" (not "Full (Strict)"). CF accepts any cert on origin when terminating TLS with the visitor. The self-signed cert is regenerated on every boot — no expiry to manage, no key rotation.

## Side effect

SSH to \`eliza-${env}-N.elizacloud.ai\` now resolves to CF IPs (proxied A record blocks the origin from DNS). Ops must SSH by **public IP** from terraform output \`control_plane_vms.<idx>.ipv4\`, not by hostname. Same pattern prod \`eliza-1\` already uses since it's also proxied in the live CF zone.

## Test plan

- [ ] CI PR validation passes (\`validate\` job runs \`terraform fmt -check\` + \`terraform validate\`)
- [ ] After merge: \`terraform apply\` on a fresh project boots a VM where:
  - \`systemctl is-active nginx\` returns active
  - \`curl -I https://<vm-ip>/\` returns 404 (= reaches agent-router which 404s on non-agent Host) — not 521 / connection refused
  - \`/etc/nginx/sites-enabled/eliza-agent-router\` exists, \`/etc/nginx/sites-enabled/default\` is gone
  - DNS record \`eliza-<env>-1.elizacloud.ai\` is proxied=true in Cloudflare

topPRs.title: feat(infra): apps-data-plane uses HCLOUD_APPS_TOKEN (shared Hetzner project)
topPRs.author: standujar
topPRs.body:
## Why

Apps Product 2 now lives in a **dedicated Hetzner project** \`apps\`, separate from the per-env agent control-plane projects (\`eliza-staging\`, \`eliza-prod\`). One shared project hosts both staging + production Apps resources — Apps is still alpha, operator overhead of two more projects (apps-staging + apps-prod) isn't worth it until there's real prod traffic to isolate.

## What

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| \`.github/workflows/terraform-apps-data-plane.yml\` | \`HCLOUD_TOKEN\` now sourced from \`secrets.HCLOUD_APPS_TOKEN\` (repo-level) instead of env-scoped \`HCLOUD_TOKEN\`. Header + preflight error msg updated. |
| \`packages/cloud-infra/cloud/terraform/hetzner/apps-data-plane/main.tf\` | Comment on the absent \`hcloud_ssh_key\` resource now explains the shared-project rationale (race + 409 between env applies). |
| \`packages/cloud-infra/cloud/terraform/hetzner/ARCHITECTURE.md\` | Multi-project diagram shows 3 projects (\`eliza-prod\`, \`eliza-staging\`, \`apps\`). Token-plumbing table adds \`HCLOUD_APPS_TOKEN\` row. |

## Topology after merge

\`\`\`
eliza-prod      env-scoped HCLOUD_TOKEN  control-plane + agents prod
eliza-staging   env-scoped HCLOUD_TOKEN  control-plane + agents staging
apps            repo-level HCLOUD_APPS_TOKEN  Apps Product 2 staging + prod (shared)
default         <legacy, to drain + delete in phase 5>
\`\`\`

## Pre-merge state

- \`HCLOUD_APPS_TOKEN\` repo secret is set (verified \`gh secret list\`)
- Operator pubkey already registered in the \`apps\` Hetzner project via Console
- \`apps\` project is empty (0 servers) — first apply will create everything

## Test plan

- [ ] CI \`validate\` job passes (terraform fmt + validate)
- [ ] After merge: \`gh workflow run terraform-apps-data-plane.yml --ref develop -f environment=staging -f action=plan\`
- [ ] Plan should show ~9 adds in \`apps\` project (network, subnet, 2 firewalls, server, server_network, volume, DNS, + tenant_db resources if provision_tenant_db=true)
- [ ] If clean: \`-f action=apply\`
- [ ] Followup (not in this PR): \`pg_dumpall\` the staging tenant DB on OLD CP → restore onto the new \`eliza-apps-tenantdb-staging\` node → update DSN refs for the 2 live user apps

## Followup (separate)

When Apps Product 2 ships to prod with real traffic, split into \`apps-staging\` + \`apps-prod\` projects (= rename existing + create second + state file split). Resource recreation not needed; it's a token-and-state-key swap.

completedItems.body:
## Summary

Three latent failures the previous tenant-db cloud-init template hits the moment a fresh server boots — all caught the hard way during the staging apply this evening. PR #8384 baked the systemd-oneshot approach; this PR makes th

completedItems.title: fix(infra): tenant-db cloud-init -> systemd oneshot (idempotent, survives rescale)
completedItems.body:
## Summary

Wraps the tenant-DB cluster setup in a systemd oneshot service installed via
`write_files`. The service runs on **every boot** (not just the first one),
every step is grep- or path-guarded so it's a no-op when there's nothing to

completedItems.title: fix(cloud): Atlas edit models take images[] + size WIDTHxHEIGHT
completedItems.body:
Follow-up to #8380. Per the Atlas image API reference:
- edit / image-to-image models take a source `images: [...]` array (not a singular `image`)
- `size` uses `WIDTH*HEIGHT` form (e.g. `2048*2048`)

Normalise a `WxH` size to that, send so

completedItems.title: feat(cloud): add Atlas Cloud image provider (drop-in, OpenAI-compatible)
completedItems.body:
## Why
The deployed cloud worker routes image models (gpt-image-2, gemini-image) through **BitRouter**, but BitRouter serves **no image models** — its `/v1/models` catalog is 100% text-output. So `generate-image` 500s. **Atlas Cloud** does

completedItems.title: refactor(infra): default tenant_db to cpx41 (shared CPU, no dedicated quota)
completedItems.body:
## Summary

Default `tenant_db_server_type` in the `apps-data-plane` Hetzner module flips from `ccx33` (dedicated 8 vCPU / 32 GB) to `cpx41` (shared 8 vCPU / 16 GB).

Why:
- ccx33 burns 8 dedicated cores from a Hetzner account-wide quota th
